title: AWS Graviton5 Reaches General Availability with 192 Cores and Formally Verified VM Isolation
gist: AWS shipped GA Graviton5 (M9g/M9gd) ARM instances on TSMC 3nm with 192 cores, DDR5-8800, PCIe Gen6, and the first formally verified hypervisor isolation in a production cloud; Meta committed tens of millions of cores, customer benchmarks show ~25-60% gains, and Graviton now exceeds half of AWS's new CPU capacity.

themes.theme: Custom silicon scale and competitive position

insights.claim: Meta has committed to deploy tens of millions of Graviton5 cores for its agentic AI efforts, making it one of the largest Graviton customers in the world, with Uber and Snowflake also deploying Graviton5.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: A tens-of-millions-of-cores commitment from Meta is a major demand validation for AWS custom silicon and a signal of hyperscaler CPU spend shifting to ARM.
insights.quote: Meta has signed on to deploy tens of millions of Graviton5 cores for its agentic AI efforts
insights.locator: The Meta commitment is the headline validation.

insights.claim: Graviton accounts for more than half of all new CPU capacity AWS added over the past three years, and AWS's custom silicon business has crossed a $20 billion annual run rate, with AWS the furthest along in custom silicon adoption versus Google's Axion and Microsoft's Cobalt 100.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: A $20B run rate and >50% of new CPU capacity quantify AWS's silicon moat and reduced reliance on x86 vendors — material for Intel/AMD and ARM-ecosystem theses.
insights.quote: the custom silicon business crossing a $20 billion annual run rate

insights.claim: Graviton5 M9g/M9gd doubles core count to 192 per chip across four chiplets on TSMC's 3nm node, with 192 MB L3 cache (5x Graviton4), DDR5-8800 (the fastest DDR5 in any cloud instance), and PCIe Gen 6.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Concrete spec leap (3nm, 192 cores, fastest cloud DDR5) sets the new performance bar competitors must answer; TSMC 3nm dependency is supply-chain relevant.
insights.quote: doubling the core count to 192 per chip and packing them onto four chiplets fabbed on TSMC's 3nm node
insights.locator: AWS recently shipped M9g and M9gd instances

insights.claim: Customer benchmarks from the six-month preview show ClickHouse +36% over M8g with zero code changes, Honeycomb +36% throughput per core over Graviton4, and HubSpot's MySQL query duration dropping up to 60%, with Airbnb calling them some of the fastest EC2 instances ever tested.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Named-customer benchmarks de-risk migration claims and accelerate enterprise adoption of ARM cloud compute.
insights.quote: ClickHouse saw a 36% performance boost compared to M8g with zero code changes
insights.locator: Customer benchmarks from the six-month preview period

insights.claim: M9g instances are uniformly 9% more expensive than M8g, which against the ~25% compute performance improvement yields roughly 15% better price-performance per dollar, though gains vary by workload.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Quantified ~15% price-performance gain is the metric that drives migration ROI decisions for cost-sensitive cloud buyers.
insights.quote: that translates to roughly 15% better price-performance per dollar
insights.locator: Pricing is published but requires context.

insights.claim: Graviton5's Nitro Isolation Engine provides mathematically proven (formally verified) isolation between VMs, making it the first major public cloud instance to carry formally verified hypervisor isolation in production — a stronger security guarantee for multi-tenant or untrusted-agent-code workloads than competing instance types.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Formally verified isolation is a differentiated security selling point especially for running untrusted AI agent code — a competitive edge in the agentic-workload race.
insights.quote: the first formally verified hypervisor isolation in a production cloud environment

insights.claim: The article argues, echoing r/hardware commenters, that calling Graviton5 an 'AI chip' overstates what changed — they are normal ARM cores and the real improvements (core count, cache, memory bandwidth, interconnect latency) benefit databases, web servers, and batch processing as much as agents.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: A caution against AI-washing of general-purpose silicon; investors should separate genuine architectural change from marketing framing.
insights.quote: The only reason those are AI cores is because somebody in marketing thinks that will sell better with the word AI attached.
insights.locator: Not everyone is buying the framing.

insights.claim: The Graviton5 family is incomplete: compute-optimized C9g and memory-optimized R9g variants are planned for later in 2026, so teams with the most demanding inference or analytics workloads may want to wait for C9g benchmarks before committing to migrate.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Upcoming C9g/R9g variants mean current M9g may not represent peak Graviton5 — timing consideration for migration and competitive benchmarking.
insights.locator: The family is not yet complete.
